source.title: Learning-related representational changes reveal dissociable integration and separation signatures in the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex

confidence_rationale: Human fMRI study of integration vs separation of related events. Relevant to MECH-285's timing question (when does integration of newly-invalidated traces happen) but not directly. The key transfer-relevant finding is that integration is more likely for established memories than for simultaneously-encoded events -- suggesting a delay between encoding and integration. Moderate confidence because the domain transfer (human fMRI, no replay measurement, no sleep manipulation) is substantial.
failure_signatures: Integration of related events into shared hippocampal representations is more likely for memories that were established prior to encoding of the related event than for events encoded simultaneously.

Anterior hippocampus and posterior MPFC integrate across memories, while posterior HPC and anterior MPFC maintain separated episodic representations; the integration process is therefore regionally partitioned, not uniform.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Anterior hippocampus and posterior medial prefrontal cortex show learning-related increases in representational similarity across related events, indicating integration, particularly when one event was established in memory prior to encoding of the related event.
mapping.ree_translation: For MECH-285's timing question: integration of newly-invalidated traces is more likely to succeed when the traces have been established (i.e., are not simultaneously being encoded). This argues against a first-bout sleep-integration reading for freshly-invalidated anchors -- the biology appears to prefer a delay between establishing a trace and integrating/revising it. In MECH-285 terms: inactive anchors that have only just been marked inactive may not yet be ready for full schema revision; a temporal-gating parameter (broad-window-N) may be architecturally appropriate.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This is human fMRI in an encoding/retrieval paradigm, not rodent sleep electrophysiology. The paper does not measure replay, does not manipulate sleep, and its 'integration' measure is a representational-similarity change in fMRI voxel patterns rather than an SWR-content measurement. The transfer to MECH-285's sleep-replay start-state distribution is therefore indirect. Use this primarily as a boundary condition: integration appears to favour established-prior traces.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI (N=32 adults); associative inference task with related event pairs encoded either sequentially (established-prior) or simultaneously.

confidence_components.notes: Good fMRI methods but the mapping to MECH-285 is weak. The paper is useful for the timing question only as a general principle (integration prefers established traces) rather than as a direct measurement of sleep-replay seed distributions.
